title: Traditional churros
subtitle: Fried choux-style pastry with cinnamon sugar
excerpt: Twenty-four churros piped from a star-tipped nozzle and fried in hot oil, then rolled in cinnamon sugar. The dough is a simple cooked paste of flour, water, and salt. Serve immediately with thick hot chocolate for dipping.
sourceNotes: Churros are documented in Spanish and Portuguese cookery from at least the seventeenth century, with the fried dough form appearing in pre-1928 Iberian household cookery texts and regional accounts of street food. The form appears in early-twentieth-century Spanish culinary periodicals and in the account of Spanish popular cookery in Angel Muro, El Practico (1894, public domain). Cross-referenced with pre-1928 Latin American cookery texts that document the spread of churros through Spanish colonial culture.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Churros do not keep. Cook and serve immediately.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried at 180°C. Hold the temperature steady; churros absorb oil if the temperature drops. Use a sugar or instant-read thermometer.

glossaryTerms.definition: Churro dough is a cooked paste: flour stirred into boiling salted water and beaten over heat until the mixture pulls away from the pan as a smooth ball. Unlike choux pastry, churro dough contains no eggs and no butter; it is the simplest possible version of a cooked flour paste. The dough is piped while still warm through a star-tipped nozzle, which gives the churros their ridged surface. The ridges increase the surface area exposed to the oil, creating more of the crisp outer crust.

content.text: Churros start with the simplest possible

: flour beaten into boiling salted water until smooth. The dough is piped directly from the bag into hot oil and fried in straight or horseshoe shapes, then rolled in cinnamon sugar. Twenty-four churros. Have everything set up before you start: the sugar ready, the oil at temperature, something to drain them on.

Bring the water and salt to a rolling boil in a medium saucepan. Tip in all the flour at once and stir vigorously over medium heat with a wooden spoon for 1 to 2 minutes until the dough forms a smooth ball and pulls cleanly away from the sides of the pan. Remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes; the dough should be warm but not too hot to handle.

Heat the oil in a large pan to 180°C. Fit the piping bag with a star tip (1.5 to 2 cm width). While the oil heats, spoon the warm dough into the piping bag. Mix the caster sugar and cinnamon together in a shallow bowl and set aside.

Pipe 10 to 12 cm lengths of dough directly into the hot oil, cutting off each churro with scissors. Fry 4 to 5 at a time for 2 to 3 minutes per side, turning once, until deep golden and cooked through. Lift out and drain briefly, then roll immediately in the cinnamon sugar. Eat while hot.
